section.title: Sing to the Lord a New Song
section.sectionRationale: A hymn of praise celebrating the LORD's upright word, His creative power in speaking the heavens into being, and His sovereign counsel that stands forever above every plan of nations and kings, closing with a plea that His loving devotion would rest on those who wait for Him.
section.thesis: The same LORD whose word spoke the heavens into existence and whose counsel stands forever above every human plan is the God whose eyes rest on those who fear Him and hope in His loving devotion.
section.frameNote: Five movements: a call to sing a new song of praise (1-3); the upright word of the LORD and His creative power (4-9); the LORD's counsel standing forever above the plans of nations (10-12); His watchful care over all humanity and the futility of trusting in military strength (13-17); and His eyes on those who fear Him, closing in a prayer that His loving devotion would rest upon them (18-22).

units.label: Sing to Him a new song

verses.text: Rejoice in the LORD, O righteous ones; it is fitting for the upright to praise Him.
Praise the LORD with the harp; make music to Him with ten strings.
Sing to Him a {{chadash|new}} song; play skillfully with a shout of joy.

units.label: By the word of the LORD the heavens were made
units.note: the upright word and creative power of God

verses.text: For the word of the LORD is upright, and all His work is trustworthy.
The LORD loves righteousness and justice; the earth is full of His loving devotion.
By the {{dabar|word}} of the LORD the heavens were made, and all the stars by the breath of His mouth.
He piles up the waters of the sea; He puts the depths into storehouses.
Let all the earth fear the LORD; let all the people of the world revere Him.
For He spoke, and it came to be; He commanded, and it stood firm.

units.label: The counsel of the LORD stands forever

verses.text: The LORD frustrates the plans of the nations; He thwarts the devices of the peoples.
The counsel of the LORD stands forever, the purposes of His heart to all generations.
Blessed is the nation whose God is the LORD, the people He has chosen as His inheritance!

units.label: He shapes the hearts of each
units.note: watchful care and the futility of human strength

verses.text: The LORD looks down from heaven; He sees all the sons of men.
From His dwelling place He gazes on all who inhabit the earth.
He shapes the hearts of each; He considers all their works.
No king is saved by his vast army; no warrior is delivered by his great strength.
A horse is a vain hope for salvation; even its great strength cannot save.

units.label: May Your loving devotion rest on us
units.note: eyes of the LORD and closing prayer

verses.text: Surely the eyes of the LORD are on those who fear Him, on those whose hope is in His {{chesed|loving devotion}}
to deliver them from death and keep them alive in famine.
Our soul waits for the LORD; He is our help and our shield.
For our hearts rejoice in Him, since we trust in His holy name.
May Your loving devotion rest on us, O LORD, as we put our hope in You.

chadash.gloss: New, fresh. A new song does not discard old songs but responds afresh to a fresh sight of God's works, an ongoing theme across the Psalter, later echoed in Revelation's new song sung before the Lamb, Revelation 5:9.

dabar.gloss: Word, speech, matter. The LORD's dabar spoke the heavens into being, the same creative Word John identifies as eternally with God and as God, through whom all things were made, John 1:1-3.

chesed.gloss: Steadfast, covenant loving devotion. The psalm both opens, verse 5, and closes, verse 22, on this quality of God, filling the earth and resting specifically on those who hope in Him.

basic.context: The psalm calls the righteous to sing a new song of praise, grounding that praise in the LORD's upright word and trustworthy work, most dramatically displayed in creation, where the heavens were made by His word and the stars by the breath of His mouth. It then turns to God's sovereignty over history, His counsel standing forever above the plans of nations, and to His watchful care over every human heart, contrasting the futility of trusting in armies and horses with the security of those who fear the LORD. It closes with a prayer that God's loving devotion would rest on those who hope in Him.
basic.hermeneutics: This psalm should be read as worship that moves naturally from creation to providence to personal hope, teaching that the same power that spoke galaxies into being is the power sustaining and directing history and personal lives, so that trust in God, not human strength, is the only sure foundation.
basic.originalLanguages: The psalm calls for a chadash, new, song, responding freshly to God's works, celebrates His dabar, word, by which the heavens were made, and closes appealing to His chesed, steadfast covenant loving devotion, both filling the whole earth and resting specifically on those who hope in Him.
basic.typology: The LORD's word by which the heavens were made, verses 6 and 9, anticipates the eternal Word identified in John 1:1-3 as with God and as God, through whom all things were made, and by whom all things continue to be sustained and hold together, Colossians 1:16-17.
basic.christ: Jesus is the Word, the dabar, by which the heavens were made, John 1:1-3, the same creative power this psalm celebrates now revealed as a person, through whom and for whom all things exist and in whom all things hold together, Colossians 1:16-17. Where the psalm declares that no king is saved by a vast army and no warrior by great strength, verse 16, Christ's kingdom is established not by earthly force but by His death and resurrection, and His counsel, unlike any earthly ruler's, truly stands forever, fulfilling verse 11 completely, Hebrews 13:8.
basic.god: God is shown as sovereign over both creation and history, His word alone sufficient to bring galaxies into being and His counsel alone able to stand firm despite every scheme of nations, while remaining intimately attentive to the hearts of every individual person He has made.
basic.crossReferences: John 1:1-3 identifies Jesus as the eternal Word through whom all things were made, fulfilling this psalm's picture of creation by God's spoken word. Colossians 1:16-17 shows Christ sustaining all created things, the ongoing fulfillment of the LORD's word holding creation firm, verse 9. Hebrews 13:8 declares Jesus Christ the same yesterday, today, and forever, the New Testament's confirmation that His counsel, unlike human plans, truly stands to all generations.
basic.oneStory: The LORD whose word spoke the heavens into being and whose counsel stands forever above every human plan reveals Himself fully in Jesus Christ, the eternal Word through whom all things were made and by whom all things are sustained, in whom God's steadfast loving devotion finally and completely rests upon all who hope in Him.

context.literary: A hymn of praise moving from a call to worship, through celebration of creation and providence, to personal trust, structured by recurring themes of God's word, His counsel, and His loving devotion framing the whole psalm.
context.historical: Likely composed for corporate worship, celebrating both the LORD's role as creator and His sovereign oversight of the nations, a message especially significant for Israel living among powerful empires whose plans could seem unstoppable.
context.canonical: Belongs to the wider creation hymn tradition of the Psalter, alongside Psalm 19 and Psalm 104, and anticipates the New Testament's fuller revelation of the Word through whom creation came to be, John 1:1-3, Hebrews 1:2-3.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To ground joyful worship in the twin realities of God's creative power and His sovereign counsel over history, teaching that trust in military strength is futile compared to trust in the LORD's steadfast loving devotion.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The psalm describes God's character and works in creation and history; it functions prescriptively as an invitation to worship freshly, to trust God's sovereignty over national and personal circumstances, and to place hope in His loving devotion rather than human strength.

debates.question: Does 'the counsel of the LORD stands forever, the purposes of His heart to all generations,' verse 11, teach that God meticulously ordains every specific human plan and decision, or does it describe a broader providential sovereignty that still leaves room for genuine human freedom within God's overarching purposes?

views.label: This verse teaches a comprehensive, meticulous sovereignty in which God's purposes govern every human plan down to the details, so that even the frustrated 'devices of the peoples,' verse 10, ultimately serve and never thwart His unchanging counsel
views.case: The parallel structure of verses 10-11 directly contrasts human plans, which are frustrated, with the LORD's counsel, which stands forever, suggesting an active, total sovereignty over the very plans described, not merely a general influence over broad historical outcomes.

views.label: The verse describes God's overarching purposes prevailing in the end over the aggregate of human schemes, without necessarily specifying that every individual human decision is itself directly ordained rather than genuinely, if finitely, free within the space God's providence allows
views.case: The psalm's focus is corporate and historical, nations and peoples rather than the metaphysics of individual choice, so the text may be read as affirming that God's overall purposes cannot ultimately be derailed by human opposition, without settling more technical questions about the nature of human freedom within that providence.

debates.bearing: Both readings affirm the psalm's central comfort: no human scheme, however powerful, can overturn what God has purposed, and this is precisely why the blessed nation, verse 12, and the trusting individual, verses 20-22, can rest secure rather than anxious.

study.originalLanguages: The psalm calls for a chadash, new, song of praise, celebrating the LORD's dabar, word, by which the heavens were made, and closes on His chesed, steadfast covenant loving devotion, both filling the earth and resting specifically on those who hope in Him.

typology.type: The word of the LORD by which the heavens were made, verses 6, 9
typology.fulfillment: Jesus, the eternal Word through whom all things were made and by whom all things hold together, John 1:1-3, Colossians 1:16-17.

typology.type: The counsel of the LORD standing forever, verse 11
typology.fulfillment: Christ's unchanging reign, Jesus Christ the same yesterday, today, and forever, Hebrews 13:8.

study.christ: Jesus is the Word, the dabar, by whom the heavens were made, John 1:1-3, the creative power this psalm celebrates now revealed as a person, through whom and for whom all things exist and in whom all things continue to hold together, Colossians 1:16-17. Where the psalm insists no king is saved by a vast army and no warrior by strength, verse 16, Christ establishes His kingdom not by force of arms but through His death and resurrection, and His counsel, unlike any human plan, truly stands forever, Hebrews 13:8.
study.god: God is shown as sovereign over both creation and history, His word alone sufficient to bring the universe into being and His counsel alone able to stand firm despite every scheme of nations, while remaining intimately attentive to the heart of every person He has made.

crossReferences.note: Jesus identified as the eternal Word through whom all things were made, fulfilling this psalm's picture of creation by God's spoken word.
Christ sustains all created things, the ongoing fulfillment of the LORD's word holding all creation firm, verse 9.
Jesus Christ the same yesterday, today, and forever, confirming that His counsel, unlike any human plan, truly stands to all generations.

study.oneStory: The LORD whose word spoke the heavens into being and whose counsel stands forever above every human plan reveals Himself completely in Jesus Christ, the eternal Word through whom all things were made and by whom all things are sustained, in whom God's steadfast loving devotion finally rests on all who hope in Him.

sermon.title: The Counsel of the Lord Stands Forever
sermon.why: Piper preaches directly from Psalm 33:10-11, arguing that God's plans, not human corporate planning stripped of Him, must be the starting point for the church, since the counsel of the LORD alone stands forever.

quiz.q: What does the psalm say was made by the word of the LORD in verse 6?

options.text: The heavens and all the stars.
options.note: This verse celebrates creation itself as accomplished by God's spoken word and the breath of His mouth.

options.text: The sky's appearance only, without reference to the stars.
options.note: Close, since the sky is part of the picture, but the verse specifically names both the heavens and all the stars, not the sky's appearance alone.

options.text: The temple in Jerusalem.
options.note: The verse describes cosmic creation, not the construction of a specific building.

options.text: Nothing; the verse is purely metaphorical about human effort.
options.note: The verse makes a direct claim about the origin of the heavens and stars through God's word.

quiz.q: According to verses 10-11, what happens to the plans of the nations compared to the LORD's counsel?

options.text: The LORD frustrates the nations' plans, but His own counsel stands forever.
options.note: This is the direct contrast the psalm draws in these two verses.

options.text: The nations' plans sometimes succeed and sometimes fail, unpredictably.
options.note: Close, since human plans do vary in outcome, but the text makes a definite claim, that the LORD actively frustrates them while His own counsel stands forever, not mere unpredictability.

options.text: The nations' plans always succeed over God's purposes.
options.note: This is the reverse of what the text states.

options.text: The LORD has no involvement in the affairs of nations.
options.note: The text explicitly describes God's active frustration of national plans, not disengagement.

quiz.q: What does the psalm say about trusting in military strength in verses 16-17?

options.text: No king is saved by a vast army, and a horse is a vain hope for salvation.
options.note: These verses explicitly declare military strength insufficient for true deliverance.

options.text: A large army helps but ultimately cannot guarantee salvation on its own.
options.note: Close to the psalm's point, but understates it; the text states flatly that no king is saved by a vast army, not merely that an army alone is insufficient.

options.text: Horses are more reliable than any other resource.
options.note: The psalm specifically calls a horse a vain hope for salvation.

options.text: Kings should avoid having any army at all.
options.note: The point is not a prohibition on having an army, but a warning against trusting in it for salvation.

quiz.q: On whom are the eyes of the LORD, according to verse 18?

options.text: Those who fear Him and hope in His loving devotion.
options.note: This verse specifies the object of God's watchful, favorable attention.

options.text: All people equally, regardless of their relationship to Him.
options.note: Close, since God's general care extends broadly, but verse 18 specifically singles out those who fear Him and hope in His loving devotion for this attentive favor.

options.text: No one in particular; God is indifferent.
options.note: This is the opposite of the verse's claim about God's attentive care.

options.text: Only the priests serving in the temple.
options.note: The verse addresses those who fear the LORD generally, not a specific priestly office.

quiz.q: What is the psalm's final request in verse 22?

options.text: That God's loving devotion would rest on His people as they put their hope in Him.
options.note: This closing prayer ties the psalm's themes of God's character and human hope together.

options.text: That God would grant military victory immediately.
options.note: The closing request concerns loving devotion and hope, not a specific military request.

options.text: That God's help would come, without specific mention of His loving devotion.
options.note: Close, since help is implied by the request, but verse 22 specifically names God's loving devotion, chesed, as the object of the psalmist's hope, not generic help.

options.text: That the psalmist would be given more wealth.
options.note: The closing prayer concerns God's loving devotion and hope, not material gain.

questions: The psalm calls for a new song in response to God's works. What recent evidence of God's power or care in your life deserves a fresh song of praise rather than a routine one?
The psalm says the heavens were made by God's word alone. How does reflecting on the sheer power of God's spoken word change the size of the problem you are currently facing?

The psalm insists no king is saved by a vast army and no horse can guarantee victory. What modern equivalents, financial security, personal competence, or reputation, do you tend to trust the way ancient armies trusted horses?
God's eyes are on those who fear Him and hope in His loving devotion. Does your daily life reflect a settled hope in God's character, or do you find your hope shifting toward circumstances instead?

The psalm closes with a prayer that God's loving devotion would rest on His people as they hope in Him. Turn that same prayer into your own words today, what do you most need God's steadfast love to cover in your life right now?

translationNotes.note: A new song appears repeatedly across the Psalter and later in Revelation 5:9, always as a fresh response to a fresh display of God's saving work, not a replacement for older songs of praise.
He spoke, and it came to be echoes Genesis 1's repeated formula, 'And God said... and it was so,' underscoring creation by divine word alone, without struggle or opposition.

Blessed is the nation whose God is the LORD reflects the unique covenant relationship of Israel, later extended in the New Testament to all who are in Christ, regardless of national origin, 1 Peter 2:9-10.
